coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots@Mg(OH)2 solid-state composites with blue, red emitting for horticultural application
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yan
Geng, Xue
Gao, Jing
Shi, Wen
Zhou, Zijing
Wang, He
Zhang, Dan
Deng, Bin
Yu, Ruijin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are being increasingly favored by researchers due to their suitable size, simple preparation method, low cost, and excellent optical performance. However, CDs are often self-quenched in the solid state, limiting their application and development. In this work, blue/red-CDs (B/R-CDs) materials are synthesized using hydrothermal method. By combining CDs with Mg(OH)2 hexagonal nanosheet in one step, a series of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 anti-self-quenching luminescent composites with dual characteristic peaks at 447 and 677 nm was then synthesized. Their photoluminescence (PL) spectra are consistent with the blue and red spectrum, which match well with the absorption of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b. The emissive colors are also tunable by various weight ratios of these composites. These materials have the excellent thermal stability and photostability. light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and light-conversion films for plant growth are further prepared and studied. Results indicate that the materials have excellent commercial horticulture applications in plant-growth lamps and light-conversion film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mg(OH)2
Carbon dots
Plant-growth LED
Light-conversion film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2-based plant-growth LED devices
Optical characteristics of B- and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites in solid state
Effects of temperature and UV irradiation time on luminescence emission
Applications of CDs@Mg(OH)2 in plant-growth LED devices
Applications of CDs@Mg(OH)2 in light-conversion film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2452019076 ), Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Northwest A&F University ( Z109021814 , Z1090219099 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 12004312 ), the Construction Program of the key discipline in Hunan Province , the Projects of the Education Department of Hunan Province (No. 18A465 ), Science and Technology Plan Project of Chenzhou city ( jsyf2017014 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images with particle-size distribution of interlayer B-CDs (a, c) and R-CDs (b, d).
SEM images of Mg(OH)2 (a), B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 (b), and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 (c).
XRD patterns of Mg(OH)2, CDs and CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites.
Infrared spectra of Mg(OH)2, CDs, and CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites.

Excitation and emission spectra of B- and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites (a, c). Excitation-independent spectra of CDs@Mg(OH)2 (b, d). Digital images of the sample under daylight (e) and 365 nm UV irradiation (f) (from left to right: B-CDs, R-CDs, Mg(OH)2, B-CDs@Mg(OH)2, and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 respectively).
The decays curves of B-CDs and B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites at 447 nm under 358 nm excitation (a), and R-CDs and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites at 677 nm under 420 nm excitation (b).
TGA curves of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites measured from 25 °C to 800 °C in N2 and air atmosphere (a, b). Effect of UV lamp irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity (c, d).
The colored contours represent the PL intensity of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites (a, b). The temperature-dependent luminescence intensities of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites (c, d).

Dual-emissive CD-based plant-growth LED-device emission spectra; inset: illustration of the schematic of the dual-emissive LED (a). Corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram and photograph of the LEDs operated at 3.0 V with UV chip (395 nm) (b).
EL spectra of LED-2 under various current conditions (a).The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram of LED under varying current conditions (b).

UV–visible transmittance spectra of light-conversion films (a). Emission spectra of light-conversion films (λ ex = 395 nm) (b). Photos of light-conversion film under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp (c, d).
Preparation route of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites and multifunctional applications in plant-growth LEDs and light-conversion films.
Different R/B mass ratios of LED packaging experiment.
The integral area and related ratios of blue and red regions in the electroluminescent spectra of LEDs at 3.0 V.
Specific film composition of different R/B mass ratios.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots@Mg(OH)2 solid-state composites with blue, red emitting for horticultural application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are being increasingly favored by researchers due to their suitable size, simple preparation method, low cost, and excellent optical performance. However, CDs are often self-quenched in the solid state, limiting their application and development. In this work, blue/red-CDs (B/R-CDs) materials are synthesized using hydrothermal method. By combining CDs with Mg(OH)2 hexagonal nanosheet in one step, a series of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 anti-self-quenching luminescent composites with dual characteristic peaks at 447 and 677 nm was then synthesized. Their photoluminescence (PL) spectra are consistent with the blue and red spectrum, which match well with the absorption of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b. The emissive colors are also tunable by various weight ratios of these composites. These materials have the excellent thermal stability and photostability. light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and light-conversion films for plant growth are further prepared and studied. Results indicate that the materials have excellent commercial horticulture applications in plant-growth lamps and light-conversion film.

ce:section.ce:para: Light is essential for plant growth, providing energy for plant growth and affecting the growth rhythm and development processes of plants [1,2]. Chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b have two typical absorption peaks, one is in the red light region in the vicinity of 660 nm, and the other is in the blue light region in the vicinity of 450 nm. Blue light is indispensable to the morphological growth of healthy plants, and red light contributes to leaf photosynthesis [3,4]. Thus, the light of these two bands is identified as “light fertilizer” for plant growth. Okamoto and Yanagi successfully conducted the first plant growth experiment using only blue and red LEDs in 1994 [5]. The use of traditional incandescent and fluorescent lamps is currently restricted in the plant growth on account of their high energy consumption and environmental pollution. Moreover, the spectra of traditional lamps cannot match the absorption spectra of plant pigments well [6]. Therefore, it will be of great benefit for commercial crop production applications if we can develop an effective method for horticulture plant-growth facilities.

With the advantages of high brightness, good reliability, excellent adjustability, low power consumption, and long lifetime, LEDs have become the primary artificial light source for plant growth and have a wide range of applications for indoor plant growth [7–9]. Agricultural light-conversion films can also significantly increase agricultural production by improving the utilization of light for crops [10]. However, rare-earth-based phosphors are nonrenewable and usually do not match the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b in the red region [11]. Meanwhile, light-conversion films based on co-assembled phosphors and polyethylene have the disadvantages of large particles, poor dispersion in resin, and release of toxic material [12,13]. Accordingly, it is necessary to study the development of new blue and red light-colored light-conversion agents.

To date, CDs show great potential in many fields, such as catalysis, corrosion inhibitor, chemical sensing, bio-imaging, nano-medicine, optoelectronic devices, LED solid-state display areas, and other fields [14–20]. Compared with rare-earth doped phosphors and colloidal semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have the advantages of excellent stability, low toxicity, common precursors, low cost, and environmental-friendly preparation methods [21,22]. However, similar to organic molecules, solid-state CDs always undergo self-quenching ascribed to excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π–π interactions, thereby limiting their practical applications in solid-state lighting [23,24]. Thus, it is of great necessity to solve self-quenching problems of CDs [25–27]. Shen et al. reported that long alkyl chains on the surface of solid CDs can suppress the quenching of luminescence caused by aggregation [28]. Aji A. Anappara et al. prepared solid carbon dots with green after-glow by embedded CDs in a silica gel matrix [29]. While Liu et al. synthesized multi-color (blue, green, green–yellow, and orange) solid CDs by one-step treatment of different kinds of CDs with boric acid [30]. However, anti-self-quenching carbon-based solid materials remain limited.

In the present work, Mg(OH)2 hexagonal nanosheets, blue emission CDs, and red emission CDs were synthesized by an one-step hydrothermal method, respectively. By introducing two B/R-CDs into the Mg(OH)2 matrix, the blue and red emission solid composites were obtained. The CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites possessed excellent luminescence and thermal stability. For the purpose of horticulture plant growth, blue and red emissive of LEDs and agricultural light-conversion films were fabricated. The emission colors can be tuned just through adjusting the proportion of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites.

B-CDs were prepared according to a recent report [31]. 0.8400 g of citric acid monohydrate and 1 mL of ethylenediamine were initially dissolved in deionized water. Then, the mixture solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the mixtures were collected and filtered through a 0.22 µm polyethersulfone membrane. The obtained yellow B-CDs solution were further purified by a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1000 Da). To remove unreacted materials and impurities with small molecular weight, this solution was dialyzed for 3 days and the deionized water was changed every 4 h. In the end, B-CDs solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C.

R-CDs were prepared according to a recent report [32]. The as-prepared precursor was developed by extracting from spinach after soaking in ethanol/water (4:1 vol ratio) and further freeze drying. Then, 0.1500 g of the green powder was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol and stirred for 10 min to form transparent solution. The mixture solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave heated 180 °C for 24 h. After the resulting solution cooled down to room temperature, the product was passed through 0.22 µm filter membranes to wipe off large black insoluble carbonaceous granules, and then solution in the membrane (MWCO = 2500 D) was dialyzed for 3 days. Finally, R-CDs solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C.

Hexagonal Mg(OH)2 nanosheets were prepared through a simple hydrothermal process. 0.2600 g of Mg(NO3)2•6H2O was added to 25 mL of deionized water, and then 0.0200 g of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) was added to the solution to decrease the hydrolysis reaction. The solution was vibrated in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min, and 0.1000 mL of ammonia solution was added dropwise to the solution. After adding 0.10 g of NaOH to the solution to keep the pH 10, it was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated 80 °C for 8 h. The reactor was cooled to room temperature, then washed with distilled water and centrifuged several times. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and white Mg(OH)2 power was obtained.

As shown in Scheme 1, B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composite material was prepared by a one-step process. 0.5000 g of Mg(OH)2 nanosheets dispersed in 10 mL of B-CDs, and the solution was stirred for 12 h vigorously at room temperature. The precipitate was removed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with deionized water, and then the composites were obtained by vacuum drying at 40 °C for 12 h. R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites was prepared by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2-based plant-growth LED devices
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare practical devices, B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were coated onto an InGaN NUV-emitting chip (λ = 395 nm). According to the specified amount in Table 1, the prepared CDs@Mg(OH)2 solid composites were thoroughly mixed with the appropriate amounts of photo-cured glues A and B at 1:4 ratio and then carefully added dropwise onto the 395 nm InGaN chip. In the end, it was cured by UV lamp for 2 h to prepare LED devices.

Ethylene vinyl acetate copolymer (EVA) thin films were synthesized as follows. About 4 g of EVA was weighed and dissolved in 30 mL of chloroform to prepare an EVA chloroform solution (9.0% by weight). After accurately weighing 0.0200 g of R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites, it was dissolved in 15 mL of chloroform to prepare an R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites chloroform solution (0.0013 g/mL). Then, 0.0050 g of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites was added to 4 mL of chloroform solution, which was sonicated for 10 min. Five sets of solutions were prepared according to the amounts in
Table 3 and stirred in a vortex mixer for 3 min. This transparent and viscous CDs–EVA solution was cautiously poured into a watch glass. The solvent was volatilized in an oven at 55 °C to obtain a film capable of emitting blue and red two-color light. The solidified CDs–EVA plate film was readily detached from the glass dish without damage.

Absorption and photoluminescence emission were measured using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The quantum efficiency was measured by FLS 980 spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere coated with BaSO4. A D2 Phaser (Bruker) powder diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα (λ = 0.15405 nm) radiation was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of CDs@Mg(OH)2 powder. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HR–TEM) were collected using Tecnai G2 Spirit Bio (FEI). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, energy spectroscopy (EDS) analysis, and the elemental mappings were collected using Nova Nano SEM-450. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Vetex70, Bruker) was used to study the functional groups within 4000–400 cm−1 through the KBr pellet method. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves were obtained with a TG209F3 Tarsus. The luminous efficiency was recorded via an integrating-sphere spectroradiometer system (HAAS2000, Everfine). Electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the fabricated LED were obtained using an HS-885 LED semi-automatic spectrophotocolorimeter.

The structure of CDs was identified by TEM. Fig. 1(a, b) show the uniform morphology of the B-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1(c, d), the size histogram and Gaussian fitting curve show that the CDs had a relatively narrow size distribution. The B/R-CDs have an average particle size of 1.63 and 3.75 nm, respectively. It can be inferred that the two kinds of CDs were successfully obtained.

Fig. 2(a–c) displays the SEM images of Mg(OH)2, B-CDs@Mg(OH)2, and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 at different magnifications. The typical SEM images show that the products are made up of thin sheets with diameters between 100 and 300 nm. A single Mg(OH)2 sheet has a thickness of about 20–40 nm with a regular hexagonal wafer structure. No obvious agglomeration phenomenon is observed, and the composite do not affect the Mg(OH)2 microstructure, indicating that the CDs were successfully dispersed uniformly between the layers, and that they easily come in contact with Mg(OH)2.

EDS analysis was used to confirm the composition of the B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites. As shown in Fig. S1(a and b), the EDS of the B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites confirms the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), magnesium (Mg), and oxygen (O) element. Elemental mappings of the sample further verified the elemental compositions. As illustrated in Fig. S1(c,d), these CDs was uniformly distributed on the surface of Mg(OH)2 in the composites.

Meanwhile, TEM and HR–TEM images of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composite were presented in Fig. S2. The results revealed that the morphology of two kinds of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composite are regular hexagonal wafer structure. Fig. S2(a–d) of HR–TEM revealed that the B/R-CDs were well dispersed on the surface of Mg(OH)2 hexagonal nanosheet. The inset of Fig. S2(b, d) showed the well-resolved lattice spacing of B-CDs and R-CDs was 0.21 nm and 0.26 nm, respectively.

The XRD patterns of the Mg(OH)2, CDs and CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites are shown in Fig. 3. The characteristic peaks at 2θ values of 18.4° (001), 37.8° (101), 50.9° (102), 58.6° (102), 61.8° (102), 68.0° (103), and 71.9° (103) well match the hexagonal Mg(OH)2 phase (PDF#84-2163). The broad diffraction peak about 2θ = 26° is considered to be the characteristic peak of the graphite species R-CDs and B-CDs [33]. This disordered carbon phase further support the existence of CDs in the composites.

Functional groups and chemical compositions were explored via FT-IR, and the spectra are shown in Fig. 4. The free hydroxyl group (–OH) stretching vibration mode appeared as a sharp peak at 3700 cm−1. The broad peak at around 3445 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of carboxylic acid, the hydroxyl functional group of the synthesized CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites. The characteristic peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric tensile vibrations of COO–Mg [34,35]. The stretching vibrations of the Mg–O bands were observed at 825 and 680 cm−1, whereas its bending modes were located at 420 cm−1 [36,37].

The high-resolution XPS spectrum (Figs. S3a and S4a) of CDs@Mg(OH)2 shows four different peaks, C1s, O1s, N1s, and Mg without other impurities. According to the elemental analysis, the content of C/O/N/Mg in the B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 is 35.62%, 25.96%, 5.56% and 32.86%, respectively. The content of C/O/N/Mg in the R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 is 38.45%, 22.89%, 6.45% and 32.21%, respectively. The binding energy peaks were at 284.3, 531.5, 49.1, and 86.7 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s, Mg2p and Mg2s, respectively [38]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Figs. S3c and S4c) has four distinct peaks. The binding energy peak at 283.87 eV presents of CC and CH (sp3) of CDs. The peaks at 284.37 eV and 285.32 eV can be assigned to CO bonded carbon and to CC species. The peak at 288.20 eV could be attributed to OCO/Mg-CO (sp2) [39]. The peak at 1301.5 eV in Figs. S3(b) and S4(b) was attributed to Mg1s. Figs. S3(d) and S4(d) show the O1s high-resolution spectrum. The three typical peaks at 530.78, 531.21, and 532.26 eV are assigned to CO, CMg, and CO bonds, respectively. [40]. The surface composition of the composites observed by XPS is good agreement with the FT-IR analysis. There are plenty of surface groups such as hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, and amino groups. These groups have a good adhesion to the Mg(OH)2 nanosheets by covalent bonds (coordination bonds) and non-covalent bonds (hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interaction).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of B- and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites in solid state
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the optical properties of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were investigated. Fig. 5(a) reveals that the excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites had symmetrical excitation and emission spectra. The maximum emission and excitation wavelengths were 447 and 358 nm, respectively. Fig. 5(b) shows the emission spectra of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites at different excitation wavelengths, where the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 310 nm to 400 nm at 10 nm intervals. The maximum emission peak position was virtually independent of the excitation wavelength, unlike previously reported B-CDs [41]. The excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were shown in Fig. 5(c). There were three characteristic excitation bands when monitored at 677 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelength was 420 nm. The emission spectra of the R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites excited at different wavelengths is shown in Fig. 5(d). With the red-shift of the excitation wavelength, the intensity was strengthened and then weakened, and the emission peaks virtually shift. These results reveal that R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites have the similar excitation wavelength-independent emission properties to B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites. The Mg(OH)2 nanosheets with the layered structure provide the limited microenvironment, and the CDs are uniformly dispersed between the layers. Thus, the B- and R-CDs are dispersed in the Mg(OH)2 medium, preventing the solid-state aggregation-induced quenching of CDs. Moreover, the H-bond interactions generated from the surface Mg(OH)2 induced the agminated CDs to present a dispersive state similar to that in liquid media [42,43]. The Mg(OH)2 matrix also neither competes for absorbing excitation light nor absorbs the emissions of CDs, leading to efficient PL emitting [44]. As shown in Fig. 5(e, f), the Mg(OH)2 and CDs separately did not have good luminescence properties, whereas the bright blue and red light emitted from CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites indicating they have good solid-state luminescence properties. Thus, we successfully prepare solid-state luminescent CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites by combining Mg(OH)2 hexagonal nanosheets and CDs.

To further understand the PL mechanisms of solid-state CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites, the PL dynamics of B- and R-CDs in water, as well as those of B- and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites, were determined, and results are shown in Fig. 6(a) and 6(b). All decay curves were well described by the following double-exponential function [45]:
(1)
I
( t )
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp ⁡ (
− t
/
τ
2
)
+
A
2
exp ⁡ (
− t
/
τ
1
)
where I(t) represents the luminescent intensity at time t, and I 0 is the background intensity, and A 1 and A 2 are the pre-exponential factors whose value is the emission intensity when t = 0. τ 1 and τ 2 are lifetime components. The average lifetime can be calculated by the following expression [46]:
(2)
τ
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2

The fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites monitored at 447 nm and the fitted lifetime τ was 14.28 ns. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites monitored at 677 nm and the fitted lifetime τ was 17.29 ns. These results indicated that the luminescence lifetime of the composites were longer than that of aqueous CD solution. The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs significantly increased due to the lower energy loss in the solid matrix than in the aqueous CD solution. Therefore, the CDs were well embedded separately in the Mg(OH)2 nanosheet matrix and effectively avoided the energy loss induced by water molecules, resulting in solid-state luminescence [43,44,47].

The IQE of luminescent materials is one key critical factor in evaluating their applicability for application in LED devices. The IQE of CDs and composites was measured and calculated based on the following equation [48],
(3)
η
=
∫
L
S
∫
E
R
−
∫
E
S
Here η is the IQE, L
S shows the emission spectrum of the studied samples, E
R and E
S present the PLE spectra of the excitation light with and without synthesized compounds, respectively. The IQE value of the as-prepared B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were found to be 42.5% and 31.7%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and UV irradiation time on luminescence emission
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7(a, b), TGA data revealed the weight loss of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites when the temperature reaches 800 °C, which exhibited excellent thermal stability from 25 °C to 400 °C. From 25 °C to 200 °C, the weight loss rates of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were 9.2% and 1.04%, respectively, owing to the removal of water embedded in the Mg(OH)2 nanosheet. The second weight loss of CDs in the range of 350–800 °C was 47.5% and 34.1%, corresponding to the dehydration of the Mg(OH)2 boundary and the disintegration of the skeleton, respectively [49]. The weight loss of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites in the N2 atmosphere was 43.2%, which was lower than that in air (47.5%). At the same time, the weight loss of R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites in the N2 atmosphere was 32.7%, which was also lower than that in air (47.5%). The possible reason was the loss of carbon content in the composite, indicating the presence of CDs in the Mg(OH)2 framework [10].

CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites should have good photostability for practical application. UV light was used as the incident light source, and B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were continuously irradiated for 240 min. As shown in Fig. 7(c, d), B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 showed no obvious change and the intensity keep in 99%, indicating that the composites had excellent ultraviolet resistance. Therefore, PL strength was stably maintained upon mixing with various materials when processed toward LEDs. These properties were essential features for LED applications.

For the application of plant-growth LEDs, the thermal-stability property should be considered. The colored contours representing the PL intensity and the temperature-dependent luminescence intensities of CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites are shown in Fig. 8. As shown in Fig. 8(a, b), no spectral shift was observed with increased temperature, the typical Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 at 303, 373, and 423 K were (0.152, 0.074), (0.152, 0.080), and (0.152, 0.087), and the CIE coordinates of R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 were (0.549, 0.449), (0.550, 0.448), and (0.551, 0.448), which confirmed the color stability of CDs@Mg(OH)2 under different temperatures.

As shown in Fig. 8(c, d), the PL intensity of CDs@Mg(OH)2 decreased slightly with increased temperature. The intensity of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 remained at 89.5% and 72.8%, corresponding to 100 °C and 150 °C compared with room temperature, respectively. For R-CDs@Mg(OH)2, PL intensity remained at 91.7% and 72.6%, corresponding to 100 °C and 150 °C compared with room temperature, respectively. Moreover, the B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2remained at 150 °C for 30 min and then further cooled to room temperature. After cooling, their PL strength restored about 89.6% and 95.8%, respectively. Comparison of thermal stability for applications in LEDs is shown in Table S1 [50, 51]. The relatively high values indicated that the CD composites materials had excellent thermal stability for potential applications in LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs@Mg(OH)2 in plant-growth LED devices
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above work, plant-growth LED devices based on a 395 nm emitting chips and B- and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were constructed. The LEDs provided blue and red illumination by controlling an appropriate mass ratio of the two types of CDs@Mg(OH)2 (Table 1). As shown in Fig. 9(a), the emission spectra of the LEDs had blue and red characteristic peaks, and the luminescence spectra have gradient change with the different mass ratios. As shown in Table 2, with R/B mass ratio change from 1:5 to 5:5, the integral area of the red light region gradually increased, whereas that of the blue light region gradually decreased, and the integral area ratio change from 73:27 to 4:96. The schematic of LEDs was shown in the inset of Fig. 9(a). With further increased R/B mass ratio from 1:5 to 4:5 and 5:5, the CIE chromaticity coordinates of LEDs exhibited a significant shift from the blue (0.185, 0.104) to white (0.352, 0.339) and orange regions (0.433, 0.375) (show in Fig. 9(b)). Photographs of the LEDs operated at 3.0 V with a UV chip are also shown in Fig. 9(b). These LEDs all had brilliant light from blue to red. LED devices with different B/R ratios can meet the various light quality and illumination required by different crops. Other related parameters of LED, such as luminous efficiency, color rendering index were also measured and shown in Table S2. Therefore, the material prepared by this simple method has potential application in the field of plant-growth LED.

The EL spectra and the variation in CIE chromaticity coordinates of the fabricated LED-2 operated under different currents (100–500 mA) were shown in Fig. 10. With increased applied current from 100 to 500 mA, the CIE chromaticity coordinates shifted slightly blue shifted from (0.254, 0.203) to (0.217, 0.158). The blue and red light intensity of the LED could be further controlled by adjusting the magnitude of the current. Therefore, the LEDs were smarter than the traditional plant growth lamp for plant growth in the field of agricultural production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs@Mg(OH)2 in light-conversion film
ce:section.ce:para: EVA is a kind of hydrophilic polymer that could produce ideal films with high light transmittance. Thus, we selected EVA, B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 to prepare EVA–CDs@Mg(OH)2 light-conversion films. The transmittance spectra of the as-synthesized sample of EVA–CDs@Mg(OH)2 light-conversion films are shown in Fig. 11(a). There is broad absorption peak in the ultraviolet regions and the maximum absorption wavelength is 278 nm due to the conjugate system in the films, which might be related to the π-π* transition of polyethylene molecules. The films maintained good transmittance (>70%) in the infrared- and visible-light regions, especially in the latter. Emission spectra of light-conversion films were shown in Fig. 11(b). The curve of films retained the same peak shape as that of CDs@Mg(OH)2. Besides, the emission spectra of films well overlapped with the absorption curves of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b. With increased R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 concentration, the intensity of the red light region gradually increased. As shown in Fig. 11(c, d), these light-conversion films had excellent transmittance under daylight. The composite emitted high-intensity blue and red emission when excited under UV light. Thus, the light-conversion films with the different B/R light ratios could also meet the various light-quality requirements of different crops. It could be well used to convert the UV-light and sunlight into the blue and red light needed for the plant growth.

The anti-self-quenching CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites were fabricated by introducing luminescent B/R-CDs into Mg(OH)2 nanosheets. The optimum emission wavelengths of B-CDs@Mg(OH)2 and R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 were at 447 and 677 nm, respectively. The spectra of the composites well matched the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b. The intensity of the CDs@Mg(OH)2 could respective retain about 89.6% and 76.8% of room temperature when warm up to 150 °C. In addition, CDs@Mg(OH)2 have excellent photostability. When continuously irradiated for 240 min, the intensity of B/R-CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites can be maintained at 99%. Based on the studies described above, dual-emissive plant-growth LEDs and light-conversion films are fabricated. By controlling the mass ratio of the two materials, the intensity ratio of the two emission peaks of PL can be adjusted. Besides, UV–visible transmittance and emission spectra show that the light-conversion film can convert ultraviolet light to blue and red light. These results indicate CDs@Mg(OH)2 composites have potential application in the field of horticulture plant growth.

Yan Xie: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xue Geng: Formal analysis. Jing Gao: Investigation. Wen Shi and Zijing Zhou: Methodology. He Wang: Software. Dan Zhang: Visualization. Bin Deng: Project administration, Resources. Ruijin Yu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2452019076), Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Northwest A&F University (Z109021814, Z1090219099), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 12004312), the Construction Program of the key discipline in Hunan Province, the Projects of the Education Department of Hunan Province (No.18A465), Science and Technology Plan Project of Chenzhou city (jsyf2017014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159663.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Cao, X. Ouyang, Y. Jiao, X. Wang, Q. Hu, T. Chen, C. Liao, Y. Li, Deep-red-emitting SrLa2Sc2O7:Mn4+ phosphor: synthesis and photoluminescence properties, J. Alloy. Compd. 795 (2019) 134–140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of N-doped carbon dots based on starch and their application in white LED
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, Y. Chen, X. Liu, Preparation of N-doped carbon dots based on starch and their application in white LED, Opt. Mater. 86 (2018) 530–536.
